examples.explanation: Start: call with index=0, last=-inf. For element 3: include -> call with index=1, last=3; skip -> call with index=1, last=-inf. Include branch: at index=1, element 10 > 3, include -> call with index=2, last=10; skip -> call with index=2, last=3. Include branch from index=1: at index=2, element 2 not > 10, so only skip -> call with index=3, last=10; this continues, eventually base cases return 0, and max lengths propagate back. The include branch from index=0 with 3 leads to subsequence [3,10,20] length 3. Skip branch from index=0 explores without 3, might get [10,20] length 2. Overall max is 3.

Start: index=0, last=-inf. For 50: include -> index=1, last=50; skip -> index=1, last=-inf. Include 50 branch: at index=1, 3 not > 50, so only skip -> index=2, last=50; this yields subsequences like [50,80] length 2. Skip 50 branch: at index=1, last=-inf, include 3 -> index=2, last=3; skip 3 -> index=2, last=-inf. Include 3 branch: at index=2, 10 > 3, include -> index=3, last=10; this can lead to [3,10,40,80] length 4. Other branches yield shorter lengths. Max is 4.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all subsequences by including or excluding each element, tracking the longest increasing one. Use it for small inputs or to understand the problem, but it's inefficient due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Longest Increasing Subsequence
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all subsequences to find the longest increasing one.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves LIS by exploring every possible subsequence of the input array. The intuition is that for each element, we have two choices: include it in the current subsequence if it maintains the increasing property, or skip it. By recursively making these decisions for all elements, we can find the maximum length among all valid increasing subsequences. The algorithm starts with an initial call where we consider including the first element or not, with no previous element selected initially. At each recursive step, we check if we can include the current element (it must be greater than the last included element, or this is the first element). If included, we move to the next index with this element as the new last included. If skipped, we move to the next index with the same last included. The base case is when we reach the end of the array, returning 0 as no more elements can be added. The result is the maximum of the include and skip branches. This works because it exhaustively checks all 2^n subsequences, ensuring we don't miss any increasing one. Time complexity is O(2^n) due to the binary branching at each element, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the recursive helper function from L1 as its core, calling it with initial parameters. Common mistakes include not handling the case where no previous element is selected (use -infinity or a flag) and missing the skip branch, which reduces completeness. Real code pattern: define a recursive function that takes current index and last value, returns max length from that point.

examples.trace: Start: call with index=0, last=-inf. For element 3: include -> call with index=1, last=3; skip -> call with index=1, last=-inf. Include branch: at index=1, element 10 > 3, include -> call with index=2, last=10; skip -> call with index=2, last=3. Include branch from index=1: at index=2, element 2 not > 10, so only skip -> call with index=3, last=10; this continues, eventually base cases return 0, and max lengths propagate back. The include branch from index=0 with 3 leads to subsequence [3,10,20] length 3. Skip branch from index=0 explores without 3, might get [10,20] length 2. Overall max is 3.

Start: index=0, last=-inf. For 50: include -> index=1, last=50; skip -> index=1, last=-inf. Include 50 branch: at index=1, 3 not > 50, so only skip -> index=2, last=50; this yields subsequences like [50,80] length 2. Skip 50 branch: at index=1, last=-inf, include 3 -> index=2, last=3; skip 3 -> index=2, last=-inf. Include 3 branch: at index=2, 10 > 3, include -> index=3, last=10; this can lead to [3,10,40,80] length 4. Other branches yield shorter lengths. Max is 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for LIS
ladders.desc: Recursive function that computes LIS length from a given index with a last included value.
ladders.explanation: This helper function is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for exploring subsequences starting from a specific position. The intuition is that at any point in the array, the LIS length depends on the current index and the last element included so far. The function takes two parameters: current index i and last value last_val (or a flag like -infinity if none). It returns the maximum length of an increasing subsequence from index i to the end, given that the last included element is last_val. The algorithm: if i equals array length, return 0 (base case). Otherwise, compute two options: skip the current element by calling recursively with i+1 and same last_val; and include the current element only if it is greater than last_val (or if last_val is -infinity, meaning first element), then call with i+1 and array[i] as new last_val, adding 1 to the result for including this element. Return the maximum of skip and include results. This works because it breaks the problem into smaller subproblems, combining results via max. Time complexity for this function alone is O(2^(n-i)) in worst case, but overall it contributes to O(2^n). Space is O(n-i) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called initially with i=0 and last_val=-inf to solve the full problem. Common mistakes: forgetting to add 1 when including, or incorrect comparison for first element. Real code pattern: define function lis_helper(i, last_val): if i==n: return 0; skip = lis_helper(i+1, last_val); include = 0; if last_val == -inf or arr[i] > last_val: include = 1 + lis_helper(i+1, arr[i]); return max(skip, include).

examples.trace: Call lis_helper(0, -inf). i=0 not end. Skip: call lis_helper(1, -inf). Include: since last=-inf, allowed, call 1+lis_helper(1,3). For skip branch: lis_helper(1,-inf): i=1, skip call lis_helper(2,-inf); include call 1+lis_helper(2,10). lis_helper(2,-inf): i=2, skip call lis_helper(3,-inf) returns 0; include allowed, 1+lis_helper(3,2) returns 1; max=1. Back to i=1: skip gets 1, include gets 1+1=2 (since 10>3? Wait, last=3 for include? Let's trace correctly: At i=0, include branch: 1+lis_helper(1,3). lis_helper(1,3): skip call lis_helper(2,3); include only if 10>3, so 1+lis_helper(2,10). lis_helper(2,3): skip call lis_helper(3,3) returns 0; include not allowed since 2 not >3, so include=0; max=0. lis_helper(2,10): skip returns 0; include not allowed since 2 not >10; max=0. So lis_helper(1,3): skip=0, include=1+0=1, max=1. Thus include branch from i=0: 1+1=2. Skip branch from i=0: lis_helper(1,-inf) as above gave max=1. Overall max=2.

Call lis_helper(0,-inf). Skip: lis_helper(1,-inf). Include: 1+lis_helper(1,1). lis_helper(1,-inf): skip lis_helper(2,-inf); include 1+lis_helper(2,2). lis_helper(2,-inf): skip lis_helper(3,-inf)=0; include 1+lis_helper(3,3)=1; max=1. So lis_helper(1,-inf): skip=1, include=1+1=2, max=2. lis_helper(1,1): skip lis_helper(2,1); include 1+lis_helper(2,2). lis_helper(2,1): skip lis_helper(3,1)=0; include since 3>1, 1+lis_helper(3,3)=1; max=1. lis_helper(2,2): skip=0; include since 3>2, 1+0=1; max=1. So lis_helper(1,1): skip=1, include=1+1=2, max=2. Back to i=0: skip branch gets 2, include branch gets 1+2=3. Max=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision Branching in Recursion
ladders.desc: The technique of making include/skip decisions at each element to explore subsequences.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the recursive decision-making process that forms the backbone of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find all subsequences, we treat each element as a binary choice: either include it in the current subsequence or skip it. This branching creates a recursion tree where each path represents a subsequence. The key is to only include an element if it maintains the increasing order relative to the last included element. This ensures we only explore valid increasing subsequences. The algorithm at each step: if the current element can be included (i.e., it's greater than the last included or there is no last included), we branch into including it (adding 1 to length and updating last value) and skipping it (keeping length and last value unchanged). If it cannot be included, we only branch into skipping it. This works because it systematically enumerates all possibilities, and by taking the maximum length from all branches, we find the LIS. Time complexity arises from the exponential number of branches, O(2^n). Space is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the helper function's include/skip calculations. Common mistakes: branching incorrectly when element cannot be included, or not considering both branches. Real code pattern: for each index i, if arr[i] > last_val: include_len = 1 + recurse(i+1, arr[i]); skip_len = recurse(i+1, last_val); return max(include_len, skip_len). Else: return recurse(i+1, last_val).

examples.trace: Given last included value is 3, current element is 5. Since 5 > 3, we can include it. Branch 1 (include): add 1 to length, set last to 5, move to next element. Branch 2 (skip): length unchanged, last remains 3, move to next element. Both branches are explored recursively.

Given last included value is 5, current element is 2. Since 2 is not > 5, we cannot include it to maintain increasing order. Thus, only one branch: skip the element. Length unchanged, last remains 5, move to next element.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal for Subsequences
ladders.desc: Using recursion to generate all subsequences of an array.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental concept of generating all subsequences recursively, which is a prerequisite for the LIS brute force approach. The intuition is that a subsequence is a selection of elements from the array in original order, and we can model this as a binary decision at each element: include or exclude it. Recursion naturally fits this by making a decision at one element and delegating the rest to a recursive call. The algorithm: define a recursive function that takes an index i and a current subsequence list. If i equals array length, process the current subsequence (e.g., print it or check properties). Otherwise, make two recursive calls: one including the current element (add to list, call with i+1) and one excluding it (call with i+1 without adding). This explores all 2^n subsequences. This works because each recursive call handles a suffix of the array, and the decisions propagate to form all combinations. Time complexity is O(2^n) to generate all subsequences, and space is O(n) for recursion stack and list storage. Connection to L2: This general subsequence generation is specialized in L2 by adding the increasing order constraint. Common mistakes: not backtracking properly when including elements, or missing the exclude branch. Real code pattern: def generate_subs(i, current): if i==n: process(current); return; generate_subs(i+1, current); generate_subs(i+1, current + [arr[i]]).

examples.trace: Start: i=0, current=[]. Call include: i=1, current=[1]. At i=1: include -> i=2, current=[1,2], process; exclude -> i=2, current=[1], process. Back to i=0: call exclude: i=1, current=[]. At i=1: include -> i=2, current=[2], process; exclude -> i=2, current=[], process. All subsequences generated.
Start: i=0, current=[]. Include: i=1, current=[3], process. Exclude: i=1, current=[], process.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Inclusion Check for Increasing Order
ladders.desc: Checking if an element can be added to a subsequence while keeping it increasing.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is a simple comparison used repeatedly in the LIS algorithm. The intuition is that for a subsequence to remain increasing, each new element must be greater than the last element in the subsequence. If no element has been added yet (i.e., the subsequence is empty), any element can be added as it starts the sequence. The operation involves comparing the current element with the last included value: if last_value is not set (often represented by negative infinity or a null flag), return true; otherwise, return true if current_element > last_value, false if not. This works because it enforces the strict increasing property (non-decreasing if >= is used, but typically LIS uses strict >). Time complexity is O(1), and space is O(1). Connection to L3: This check is applied in the subsequence generation to filter only increasing ones. Common mistakes: using >= instead of > for strict increasing, or not handling the empty subsequence case. Real code pattern: def can_include(current, last): if last is None or last == -inf: return True; return current > last.

examples.trace: Last value is 3, current element is 5. Since 5 > 3, the check returns true, meaning 5 can be included.
Last value is 5, current element is 2. Since 2 is not > 5, the check returns false, meaning 2 cannot be included without breaking increasing order.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences
ladders.desc: The basic idea of what a subsequence is and how it differs from subarrays.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is essential for grasping the LIS problem. A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from another sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. Unlike a subarray, which must be contiguous, a subsequence can have gaps between elements. For example, from the array [1,2,3,4], [1,3,4] is a subsequence but not a subarray. The intuition is that subsequences represent selections of elements in their original relative order, which is key in problems like LIS where we care about order but not necessarily adjacency. To generate subsequences, think of each element as having two options: include or exclude, leading to 2^n total subsequences for an array of length n. This works because each combination of decisions yields a valid subsequence. Time and space are not typically analyzed here as it's a conceptual understanding. Connection to L4: This concept underlies the operation of checking inclusion in a subsequence. Common mistakes: confusing subsequences with subarrays or subsets (which don't preserve order). Real code pattern: conceptually, no code, but understand that subsequences maintain order.

examples.trace: From [A,B,C], by including or excluding each element: exclude all gives []; include only A gives [A]; include only B gives [B]; include only C gives [C]; include A and B gives [A,B]; include A and C gives [A,C]; include B and C gives [B,C]; include all gives [A,B,C]. All preserve original order.
Options: exclude both -> []; include only 1 -> [1]; include only 2 -> [2]; include both -> [1,2]. Note [2,1] is not a subsequence because order changed.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n²)
approaches.summary: Use a DP array where each element stores the LIS length ending at that index, computed by checking all previous elements. This is a standard bottom-up approach suitable for medium-sized arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Increasing Subsequence with DP O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP array where each element stores LIS length ending at that index
ladders.explanation: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem asks: given an array of integers, find the length of the longest subsequence where elements are strictly increasing. A subsequence maintains order but can skip elements. The DP O(n²) approach solves this by building a DP array 'dp' where dp[i] represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i. The intuition: to compute dp[i], we look at all previous indices j < i. If nums[j] < nums[i], then we can extend the subsequence ending at j by including nums[i], giving potential length dp[j] + 1. We take the maximum over all such j. Initialize all dp[i] = 1 since each element alone forms a subsequence of length 1. The final answer is max(dp). This works because we consider all possible ending points and build optimal solutions bottom-up. Time: O(n²) from nested loops. Space: O(n) for dp array. Connection to L1: This uses the core recurrence relation repeatedly. Common mistakes: forgetting to initialize dp to 1, using ≤ instead of < for increasing check, not considering all j < i. Real pattern: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1 for j in range(i) if nums[j] < nums[i]) else 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing DP[i] for a Given Index
ladders.desc: Calculate the LIS length ending at index i using all previous indices
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes dp[i], the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending exactly at index i. The intuition: to find the best subsequence ending at i, we consider all previous positions j where nums[j] < nums[i]. For each such j, we can append nums[i] to the subsequence ending at j, giving length dp[j] + 1. We take the maximum over all valid j, or 1 if no such j exists (the element alone). This captures the optimal substructure: the optimal solution ending at i builds on optimal solutions ending at earlier indices. Step-by-step: Initialize best = 1. For j from 0 to i-1: if nums[j] < nums[i], update best = max(best, dp[j] + 1). Set dp[i] = best. Time: O(i) for each i, O(n²) total. Space: O(1) extra for this computation. Connection to L0: This is the inner loop of the full solution. Common mistakes: not handling empty previous indices, using ≤ instead of <. Real pattern: best=1; for j in range(i): if nums[j]<nums[i]: best=max(best, dp[j]+1).

examples.trace: Given nums=[3,1,4,2], i=3 (element 2), dp so far: dp[0]=1 (for 3), dp[1]=1 (for 1), dp[2]=2 (for 4). Compute dp[3]: best=1. j=0: nums[0]=3 >= 2, skip. j=1: nums[1]=1 < 2 → dp[1]+1=2, best=max(1,2)=2. j=2: nums[2]=4 >= 2, skip. dp[3]=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for LIS DP
ladders.desc: The mathematical relation: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1 for all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]) or 1
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation that defines the DP solution: dp[i] = max(1, max{dp[j] + 1 | 0 ≤ j < i and nums[j] < nums[i]}). This relation captures the essence of the problem. Intuition: The LIS ending at i either consists of just nums[i] (length 1), or it extends some previous LIS ending at j where nums[j] < nums[i]. By taking the maximum over all valid j, we ensure optimality. This works because of optimal substructure: an optimal solution to the subproblem (LIS ending at i) contains optimal solutions to smaller subproblems (LIS ending at j). The base case is dp[i] = 1 for all i initially. Step-by-step: For each i, iterate j from 0 to i-1, apply the condition, compute potential lengths, and take max. Time per i: O(i). Space: O(n) for dp. Connection to L1: This relation is implemented in the sub-routine. Common mistakes: misunderstanding strict vs non-strict increasing, not including the 1 case. Real pattern: if nums[j] < nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Previous Indices with Condition
ladders.desc: Loop through all indices j < i and check if nums[j] < nums[i]
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating over all previous indices j from 0 to i-1 and applying a condition: nums[j] < nums[i]. The intuition: to extend a subsequence, we need strictly smaller previous elements. This iteration explores all possible predecessors for the current element i. It's a fundamental pattern in DP where we look back at earlier solutions. Step-by-step: For a fixed i, set up a loop for j in range(0, i). Inside, check if nums[j] < nums[i]. If true, we can consider extending from j. This operation is repeated for each i in the outer loop. Time: O(i) for each i. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This implements the 'for all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]' part of the recurrence. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop range, using ≤ instead of <. Real pattern: for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: ...

examples.trace: i=3 (element 3). j=0: nums[0]=4 >= 3, skip. j=1: nums[1]=2 < 3, valid. j=2: nums[2]=5 >= 3, skip. So only j=1 is valid.
i=3 (element 3). j=0: 1<3, valid. j=1: 4>=3, skip. j=2: 2<3, valid. Valid j indices: 0 and 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements for Increasing Order
ladders.desc: Check if one element is strictly less than another in an array
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two elements in an array to determine if they are in strictly increasing order: nums[j] < nums[i]. The intuition: for a subsequence to be increasing, each element must be less than the next. This comparison is the fundamental condition used throughout the LIS algorithm. It ensures we only extend subsequences when the new element is larger. Step-by-step: Given indices j and i, access nums[j] and nums[i], then evaluate the boolean expression nums[j] < nums[i]. If true, it means we can place nums[i] after nums[j] in an increasing sequence. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This condition is applied during iteration over previous indices. Common mistakes: using ≤ (non-strict) instead of < (strict), comparing values instead of indices. Real pattern: if nums[j] < nums[i]: ...

examples.trace: nums[0]=5, nums[1]=10. Check 5 < 10: true.
nums[0]=8, nums[1]=8. Check 8 < 8: false (not strictly less).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its index position
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves accessing an element in an array by its index. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each with a unique index starting from 0. To access an element, you use the syntax array[index], which returns the value at that position. Intuition: Arrays allow random access, meaning you can directly get any element if you know its index, in O(1) time. This is crucial for the LIS algorithm because we frequently need to compare elements at specific positions. Step-by-step: Given an array 'nums' and an integer index i, ensure i is within bounds (0 ≤ i < length of nums). Then, nums[i] gives the element. For example, if nums = [10,20,30], nums[0] = 10, nums[1] = 20. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This operation is used to get values for comparison. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real pattern: value = nums[i].

examples.trace: Array nums has indices 0,1,2. At index 1, the element is 2.
Single-element array. Index 0 gives the first and only element: 100.

approaches.name: Greedy with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain an active list of smallest possible tail elements for increasing subsequences of various lengths, updating it greedily using binary search. This is the most efficient approach for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Increasing Subsequence using Greedy with Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete O(n log n) solution for finding the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence in an array.
ladders.explanation: The greedy with binary search approach solves LIS in O(n log n) time by maintaining an 'active list' of smallest possible tail elements for increasing subsequences of various lengths. Intuitively, for each element in the input array, we either extend an existing subsequence (if the element is larger than all tails) or replace an existing tail with a smaller value (making future extensions easier). This greedy replacement strategy ensures we always have the most favorable tails for building longer subsequences. The algorithm works by iterating through the array: for each element, we use binary search to find its position in the active list (the smallest index where tails[index] ≥ element). If the element is larger than all tails, we append it (extending LIS length). Otherwise, we replace tails[pos] with the element (maintaining same length but with better tail). The final length of the tails list equals the LIS length. Time complexity is O(n log n) because we perform binary search (O(log n)) for each of n elements. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the tails list. This approach is optimal for large inputs where O(n²) DP would be too slow. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty arrays, confusing strictly increasing with non-decreasing, or incorrectly implementing binary search bounds. The key insight is that we only care about subsequence lengths, not the actual subsequences, allowing us to greedily replace tails.

examples.trace:
Input: [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18]
Initialize tails = []
Process 10: tails empty → append 10 → tails = [10]
Process 9: binary search finds pos=0 (tails[0]=10 ≥ 9) → replace tails[0] with 9 → tails = [9]
Process 2: binary search finds pos=0 (tails[0]=9 ≥ 2) → replace tails[0] with 2 → tails = [2]
Process 5: binary search finds pos=1 (5 > all tails) → append 5 → tails = [2, 5]
Process 3: binary search finds pos=1 (tails[1]=5 ≥ 3) → replace tails[1] with 3 → tails = [2, 3]
Process 7: binary search finds pos=2 (7 > all tails) → append 7 → tails = [2, 3, 7]
Process 101: binary search finds pos=3 (101 > all tails) → append 101 → tails = [2, 3, 7, 101]
Process 18: binary search finds pos=3 (tails[3]=101 ≥ 18) → replace tails[3] with 18 → tails = [2, 3, 7, 18]
Final tails length = 4 → LIS length = 4 (e.g., [2, 3, 7, 18] or [2, 3, 7, 101])

Input: [0, 1, 0, 3, 2, 3]
Initialize tails = []
Process 0: tails empty → append 0 → tails = [0]
Process 1: binary search finds pos=1 (1 > all tails) → append 1 → tails = [0, 1]
Process 0: binary search finds pos=0 (tails[0]=0 ≥ 0) → replace tails[0] with 0 → tails = [0, 1]
Process 3: binary search finds pos=2 (3 > all tails) → append 3 → tails = [0, 1, 3]
Process 2: binary search finds pos=2 (tails[2]=3 ≥ 2) → replace tails[2] with 2 → tails = [0, 1, 2]
Process 3: binary search finds pos=3 (3 > all tails) → append 3 → tails = [0, 1, 2, 3]
Final tails length = 4 → LIS length = 4 (e.g., [0, 1, 2, 3])

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search in Active Tails List
ladders.desc: Find insertion position of element in sorted tails array using binary search.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core optimization that makes the LIS algorithm O(n log n). Given a sorted array 'tails' and a target element, we need to find the smallest index 'pos' where tails[pos] ≥ target (for strictly increasing LIS). This is essentially a lower_bound binary search. Intuitively, we're searching for where this element would fit in the sorted sequence: if it's larger than all existing tails, it extends the longest subsequence; otherwise, it replaces an existing tail at the found position to maintain the greedy property. The algorithm uses standard binary search with left=0, right=len(tails). While left < right, compute mid = (left+right)//2. If tails[mid] < target, search right half (left = mid+1); otherwise, search left half including mid (right = mid). Return left as the insertion position. Time complexity is O(log n) where n is current tails length. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each array element. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in bounds, confusing < with ≤, or not handling empty lists. The key is that we want the first position where the element is ≥ target, not > target, to handle strictly increasing sequences correctly.

examples.trace:
tails = [2, 5, 7], target = 3
Initialize left=0, right=3
Iteration 1: left=0, right=3 → mid=1 → tails[1]=5 ≥ 3 → right=mid=1
Iteration 2: left=0, right=1 → mid=0 → tails[0]=2 < 3 → left=mid+1=1
Now left=1, right=1 → loop ends
Return left=1
Position 1 is where 3 would replace 5 (since tails[1]=5 ≥ 3)

tails = [1, 4, 6], target = 8
Initialize left=0, right=3
Iteration 1: left=0, right=3 → mid=1 → tails[1]=4 < 8 → left=mid+1=2
Iteration 2: left=2, right=3 → mid=2 → tails[2]=6 < 8 → left=mid+1=3
Now left=3, right=3 → loop ends
Return left=3
Position 3 is beyond array end, meaning 8 > all tails, so append

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Tail Replacement Strategy
ladders.desc: Maintain smallest possible tail elements for each subsequence length to maximize future extensions.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the O(n log n) LIS algorithm. Instead of tracking all possible subsequences (exponential), we maintain only one candidate tail for each possible subsequence length. The key invariant: tails[i] stores the smallest possible tail element for an increasing subsequence of length i+1. When processing a new element, we compare it with tails: if it's larger than all tails, we can extend the longest subsequence by appending it. If it's not larger, we find where it would fit in tails (using binary search) and replace that position's element with the new one if it's smaller. This greedy replacement works because: 1) Replacing with a smaller element doesn't affect existing subsequences of that length (we still have a valid subsequence ending with the new element). 2) It makes future extensions easier since smaller tails are more likely to be extended. The algorithm guarantees that tails remains sorted, and its final length equals the LIS length. Time per element is O(log n) for binary search, total O(n log n). Space is O(n). This feeds into L1 by providing the context for binary search. Common mistakes include not understanding why replacement is valid, or trying to reconstruct the actual subsequence from tails (which isn't possible with this method). The intuition is similar to maintaining the most 'promising' candidates for each length.

examples.trace:
Current tails: [2, 5] represents:
- Length 1 subsequence with smallest tail 2
- Length 2 subsequence with smallest tail 5
New element: 3
Binary search finds position 1 (since tails[1]=5 ≥ 3)
Replace tails[1] with 3 → tails = [2, 3]
Why valid? We had a length 2 subsequence ending with 5 (e.g., [2,5]). Now we have a length 2 subsequence ending with 3 (e.g., [2,3]). The length 2 subsequence with tail 3 is more promising because 3 < 5, making it easier to extend with future elements >3.

Current tails: [2, 3, 7] represents lengths 1,2,3
New element: 8
Binary search finds position 3 (8 > all tails)
Append 8 → tails = [2, 3, 7, 8]
Why valid? 8 extends the length 3 subsequence ending with 7 to create a new length 4 subsequence ending with 8. This increases LIS length from 3 to 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Maintaining Sorted Array Invariant
ladders.desc: Keep an array sorted while processing elements with binary search and in-place updates.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves maintaining a dynamically growing sorted array. We start with an empty array 'tails'. For each new element, we find its correct position using binary search (O(log n)), then either append it (if position is at end) or replace the element at that position (if position is within array). The key invariant is that 'tails' remains sorted in increasing order after each operation. This works because: 1) When we append at the end, the new element is larger than all existing elements, preserving sorted order. 2) When we replace at position pos, the new element is ≤ the old element at that position (since binary search finds first position where tails[pos] ≥ element), and it's ≥ the element at pos-1 (since tails[pos-1] < element, otherwise binary search would have returned pos-1). Thus sorted order is maintained. Time per operation is O(log n) for search plus O(1) for append/replace. This feeds into L2 by enabling the greedy tail replacement. Common mistakes include not verifying the invariant, or using linear search instead of binary search. The intuition is similar to inserting into a sorted list while keeping it sorted, but with replacement instead of insertion when the element isn't new-largest.

examples.trace:
Initial sorted tails: [2, 5]
Find position for 3 using binary search:
- Compare with middle element 5: 3 < 5, so search left half
- Compare with 2: 3 > 2, so position is 1
Replace tails[1] with 3 → tails = [2, 3]
Verify sorted: 2 ≤ 3 ✓
Invariant maintained: array still sorted

Initial sorted tails: [1, 4, 6]
Find position for 8 using binary search:
- Compare with middle element 4: 8 > 4, search right
- Compare with 6: 8 > 6, position is 3 (end)
Append 8 → tails = [1, 4, 6, 8]
Verify sorted: 1 ≤ 4 ≤ 6 ≤ 8 ✓
Invariant maintained

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Search on Array
ladders.desc: Find position of target in sorted array using divide-and-conquer.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is a fundamental algorithm that finds the position of a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. Intuitively, it works like looking up a word in a dictionary: you open to the middle, see if your word comes before or after, then repeat on the appropriate half. For LIS, we need a variant that finds the first position where array[pos] ≥ target (lower_bound). The algorithm: initialize left=0, right=len(array). While left < right, compute mid = (left+right)//2. If array[mid] < target, set left = mid+1 (target is in right half). Otherwise, set right = mid (target is at mid or in left half). When loop ends, left equals the insertion position. Time complexity is O(log n) as we halve search space each iteration. Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the efficient search needed to maintain sorted order. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in bounds, infinite loops from incorrect midpoint calculation, or confusion between < and ≤. The key is that we're searching for position, not just existence.

examples.trace:
array = [2, 5, 7], target = 3
left=0, right=3
Iteration 1: mid=(0+3)//2=1 → array[1]=5
5 ≥ 3 → right=mid=1
Iteration 2: left=0, right=1 → mid=0 → array[0]=2
2 < 3 → left=mid+1=1
Now left=1, right=1 → loop ends
Return left=1
Position 1 is where 3 would be inserted

array = [1, 4, 6], target = 8
left=0, right=3
Iteration 1: mid=1 → array[1]=4
4 < 8 → left=mid+1=2
Iteration 2: left=2, right=3 → mid=2 → array[2]=6
6 < 8 → left=mid+1=3
Now left=3, right=3 → loop ends
Return left=3
Position 3 is beyond array end

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Increasing Subsequences
ladders.desc: Understand what an increasing subsequence is and how to identify them.
ladders.explanation: An increasing subsequence is a sequence of elements from an array that appear in the same order as in the array, with each element being strictly greater than the previous one. For example, in array [3,1,4,2], [1,4] is an increasing subsequence (elements at indices 1 and 2), but [3,1] is not (since 1 < 3). The 'longest increasing subsequence' (LIS) is the subsequence with maximum length. Key points: 1) Subsequence means we can skip elements (unlike subarray which must be contiguous). 2) Strictly increasing means each element must be greater than the previous (not equal). 3) There can be multiple LIS of same length. To find one manually: look for elements that form an ascending chain while preserving order. For array [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18], one LIS is [2,5,7,101] (length 4). This foundational concept is prerequisite for LIS algorithms. Time to check if a sequence is increasing is O(k) for length k. This feeds into L4 by defining what we're searching for. Common mistakes include confusing subsequence with subarray, or allowing equal elements (non-decreasing). The intuition is like picking cards from a deck in order, where each next card must have higher value.

examples.input: Array [3,1,4,2], check if [1,4] is increasing subsequence
examples.trace:
Array: [3, 1, 4, 2]
Candidate subsequence: [1, 4]
Step 1: Find indices in array: 1 at index 1, 4 at index 2 → order preserved ✓
Step 2: Check increasing: 1 < 4 ✓
Therefore [1,4] is a valid increasing subsequence.
Note: [3,4] is also valid (indices 0 and 2, 3 < 4).

examples.input: Array [5,2,8,6], check if [2,6,8] is increasing subsequence
examples.trace:
Array: [5, 2, 8, 6]
Candidate: [2, 6, 8]
Step 1: Find indices: 2 at index 1, 6 at index 3, 8 at index 2 → order not preserved (8 comes before 6 in array)
Therefore invalid (not a subsequence).
Even if order were preserved, 6 < 8 ✓, but order violation makes it invalid.
